question: What concurrency hazard does this code exhibit?
options: Classic lock-ordering deadlock: t1 takes A then B while t2 takes B then A
Data race on the inner i32
explain: Reviewing for deadlock requires checking that all threads acquire multiple locks in a consistent global order. Here t1 locks a then b and t2 locks b then a, the textbook two-lock deadlock. Rust's Mutex is not reentrant and offers no deadlock detection.

question: What is the backpressure behavior of sync_channel(0) here vs an unbounded channel()?
options: sync_channel(0) is a rendezvous: each send blocks until a matching recv, unlike unbounded channel() which never blocks the sender
explain: A reviewer must distinguish zero-capacity rendezvous channels from unbounded ones. With capacity 0 each send blocks until a recv pairs with it, providing strict backpressure; channel() returns immediately from send.

question: What is wrong with using Relaxed on the flag pattern here?
options: Nothing - Relaxed is fine for booleans
Relaxed lacks synchronizes-with semantics: the reader may observe flag=true but a stale data value; the flag store needs Release and load needs Acquire
Relaxed forces a full fence on x86 anyway, so it's wasted
explain: Reviewing atomic code requires distinguishing memory orderings. Relaxed provides atomicity but no inter-thread ordering, so prior non-atomic or Relaxed writes may not be visible. The flag-publish idiom requires Release on store and Acquire on the spinning load to establish a happens-before edge.

question: What hazard appears when this runs on a writer-preferring RwLock implementation?
options: No hazard - read guard is released before write
Reader-to-writer upgrade deadlock: r is still alive when write() is called on the same thread, and std::sync::RwLock does not guarantee recursive read+write on one thread
RwLock is reentrant in std so always safe
write() returns an Err but never blocks
explain: std::sync::RwLock makes no guarantee about acquiring a write lock while the same thread holds a read guard; on writer-preferring implementations this self-deadlocks. The reviewer should require the read guard to be dropped before requesting write.

question: What is the defect?
options: Barrier was constructed for 3 participants but only 2 threads call wait(); all threads block forever
Barrier::wait can only be called once per Barrier
explain: Barrier::new(N) requires exactly N wait() calls to release. Spawning only 2 threads against a barrier of 3 means none of them ever advance. Reviewers should always match Barrier capacity to the actual rendezvous count.

code:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	ch := make(chan int)
	ch <- 1
	fmt.Println(<-ch)
}
question: What is the runtime behavior of this program?
explain: An unbuffered channel synchronizes sender and receiver; the send blocks until a receiver is ready. Since there is only one goroutine, no receiver can ever run, producing an all-goroutines-asleep fatal deadlock. Reviewers must flag any unbuffered send that lacks a paired receiver in another goroutine.

question: What does the range loop do after the channel is drained?
options: Blocks forever waiting for new values
Panics because the channel is closed
Exits the loop cleanly after printing 1 and 2
explain: A range over a channel terminates when the channel is closed AND drained, yielding all buffered values first. This is the idiomatic shutdown pattern for producer/consumer loops. A reviewer should verify the channel is closed exactly once by the sole producer to make range terminate.

question: What happens at runtime?
options: The send delivers 1 to the goroutine
The close is a no-op on an empty channel
explain: Sending on a closed channel always panics; closing must be done by the send side, never the receive side, and never before final sends. A reviewer should ensure a single owner closes the channel and that no producer can still race a send after close.

question: What is the outcome of executing this select?
options: Deadlocks because both cases on a nil channel block forever
Panics with a nil channel dereference
explain: Operations on nil channels block forever, so a select with only nil-channel cases (and no default) deadlocks. This is intentionally used to disable a case dynamically by nilling its channel. Reviewers should flag uninitialized channel variables that bypass make.

question: What concurrency-control pattern does the buffered channel implement?
options: A barrier that waits for all goroutines to finish
A counting semaphore capping concurrent goroutines at 4
explain: A buffered channel of capacity N acts as a counting semaphore: a send acquires a slot (blocking when full), and a receive releases one. This bounds concurrency without external libraries.

question: What is the primary security flaw in this function?
options: User input is concatenated into dynamic SQL allowing injection
plpgsql is slower than SQL functions
explain: The term parameter is concatenated directly into the EXECUTE string, enabling classic SQL injection. The reviewer must recognize dynamic SQL composition and require USING $1 with format placeholders or quote_literal().

question: How should the user-controlled ORDER BY column be handled?
options: Validate against an allowlist of column names or use quote_ident with whitelist
ORDER BY cannot be injected so it is safe
explain: Identifiers in ORDER BY cannot be parameterized via bind variables; quote_literal would corrupt them. Reviewers must require an allowlist check before interpolation, since attackers can append subqueries or expose data via expression-based ordering.

question: What is wrong with the search_path configuration?
options: SET LOCAL has no effect inside functions
pg_temp is listed first, allowing an attacker who created a same-named object in their temp schema to hijack resolution in a SECURITY DEFINER context
public should never be on the search_path
explain: Putting pg_temp before trusted schemas in a SECURITY DEFINER function lets any caller shadow employees, functions, or operators with malicious copies. Reviewers must enforce SET search_path = pg_catalog, public, pg_temp (pg_temp last) or fully qualify all object references.

question: What is missing from this RLS policy for full tenant isolation?
options: No corresponding policy covers INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE, allowing writes that bypass the SELECT filter and FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is not enabled for table owners
RLS is only valid on partitioned tables
current_user_id is not a built-in function
explain: A SELECT-only policy permits writes when no USING/WITH CHECK exists for those commands; table owners also bypass RLS unless FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is set. The reviewer must require policies per command and ALTER TABLE ... FORCE RLS.

question: Why is plain = unsafe for comparing secrets/tokens?
options: String equality short-circuits on the first differing byte, leaking information about the secret via response timing; constant-time comparison is required
explain: PostgreSQL's text comparison is short-circuit and observable via timing in remote attacks. The reviewer must require a constant-time comparison.

code:
const x = null;
console.log(typeof x);
question: What does this log?
explain: typeof null returns 'object' due to a historical JavaScript bug preserved for backward compatibility. Reviewers should flag any null-check using typeof === 'object' as buggy since it incorrectly accepts null.

question: What is the output?
explain: NaN is the only JavaScript value not equal to itself, so n === n is false. Reviewers should require Number.isNaN (not === or the global isNaN which coerces) for NaN detection.

code:
const result = [] == ![];
console.log(result);
question: What does this evaluate to?
explain: ![] is false; then [] == false coerces both sides to numbers: [] to '' to 0 and false to 0, so 0 == 0 is true. Reviewers should ban == in favor of === to avoid these multi-step coercion traps.

question: What is the result?
explain: delete removes the element but preserves length, leaving a hole that reads as undefined. Reviewers should require splice for true removal so length and density stay consistent.

question: What is logged?
explain: JSON round-tripping flattens Dates to ISO strings and Maps to {}, while structuredClone preserves their prototypes. Reviewers should reject JSON-based deep clone for any data containing Date, Map, Set, or typed arrays.

question: What is logged?
explain: Set membership uses SameValueZero, so NaN equals NaN and +0 equals -0, collapsing both duplicates. Reviewers should understand that === semantics differ from Set/Map key equality.

question: How do you make this exponential recursion fast without rewriting it?
options: Decorate with functools.lru_cache(maxsize=None) to memoize calls
explain: Naive recursive fib is O(2^n) due to overlapping subproblems. functools.lru_cache transparently memoizes, collapsing the call tree to O(n) calls.

code:
def has_any(items, targets):
    return any(t in items for t in targets)
question: If items is a large list and targets has many entries, what is the membership-test issue?
options: 'in' on a list is O(n); convert items to a set once for O(1) lookups
any() short-circuits so it is already optimal
Generator expressions are slower than list comprehensions here
Should use filter() instead of any()
explain: Membership testing on a list is linear, so the call is O(len(items)*len(targets)). Converting items to a set once gives amortized O(1) per lookup, reducing total work to O(len(items)+len(targets)).

question: What collection swap improves asymptotic complexity?
options: Use collections.deque and popleft(); list.pop(0) is O(n) while deque.popleft is O(1)
explain: list.pop(0) shifts all remaining elements left, making the loop O(n^2). collections.deque provides O(1) popleft and is the right structure for FIFO drains.

code:
def insert_sorted(seq, x):
    seq.append(x)
    seq.sort()
    return seq
question: If seq is already sorted, what is the correct insertion call?
options: bisect.insort(seq, x) is O(n) for the shift but avoids the O(n log n) resort
explain: Re-sorting after every append is O(n log n) per insertion. bisect.insort binary-searches the insertion point in O(log n) and only pays the unavoidable O(n) list shift.

code:
def top_k(scores, k):
    return sorted(scores, reverse=True)[:k]
question: For small k relative to len(scores), what is the standard speedup?
options: heapq.nlargest(k, scores) runs in O(n log k) instead of O(n log n)
explain: Full sort is O(n log n); for k much smaller than n, heapq.nlargest maintains a heap of size k for O(n log k). It is the canonical top-k answer in Python reviews.

code:
import ast
user_input = 'expr'
result = eval(user_input)
question: What is the primary security vulnerability in this code?
options: Use of input() instead of raw_input()
explain: eval() executes arbitrary Python expressions, allowing RCE when fed untrusted input. A reviewer should flag any eval() on user data and recommend ast.literal_eval() or a proper parser.

question: What should this code use instead?
options: yaml.safe_load to avoid arbitrary object construction
explain: yaml.load with the default FullLoader/Loader can instantiate arbitrary Python objects via tags like python/object, enabling RCE. Reviewers must require yaml.safe_load (or Loader=SafeLoader) for any externally sourced YAML.

question: Why is SHA-256 still inappropriate here?
options: No salt and no work factor - vulnerable to rainbow tables and GPU brute force
explain: Even cryptographically strong hashes are unsuitable for passwords without a unique salt and a tunable work factor; SHA-256 is too fast and enables offline cracking. Reviewers should require bcrypt/scrypt/Argon2 or at minimum PBKDF2-HMAC with a high iteration count and per-user salt.

question: What should replace random here?
options: secrets.token_hex(16) - random module is not cryptographically secure
explain: random uses a deterministic Mersenne Twister whose state can be recovered from a few outputs, making tokens predictable. Reviewers should require secrets.token_hex/token_urlsafe (CSPRNG) for any security-relevant token.

code:
import zipfile
def unpack(archive, dest):
    with zipfile.ZipFile(archive) as z:
        z.extractall(dest)
question: Why is extractall risky?
options: Zip entries with '../' or absolute paths can write outside dest (Zip Slip)
explain: ZipFile.extractall trusts entry names; crafted archives with '../' components write arbitrary files outside dest (Zip Slip). Reviewers should iterate entries and validate that the resolved path stays within the destination before extracting.

question: What is the consequence?
options: Signature is not checked - attacker forges arbitrary claims
explain: Disabling verify_signature accepts any token regardless of signature, allowing an attacker to mint admin claims. Reviewers must require signature verification, an explicit algorithms whitelist, and rejection of 'none'.

question: What is missing?
explain: target=_blank without rel=noopener lets the opened page access window.opener and redirect the original tab (reverse tabnabbing). Reviewers should require rel=noopener noreferrer.

question: What is wrong with this cookie?
options: SameSite=None requires the Secure attribute or browsers reject it
explain: Modern browsers refuse SameSite=None cookies without Secure, and shipping cross-site cookies over HTTP exposes them. Reviewers should require Secure whenever SameSite=None is used.

question: What is the issue?
options: String === enables timing attacks on MAC comparison
explain: Native string comparison short-circuits on the first mismatch, leaking byte positions over time. Reviewers should require crypto.timingSafeEqual on equal-length Buffers.

question: What is the critical issue?
options: algorithm 'none' accepts unsigned tokens, bypassing auth
explain: Allowing alg=none lets attackers forge tokens with no signature. Reviewers must restrict the algorithms allow-list to the exact expected algorithm such as HS256 or RS256.

code:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var m map[string]int
	_ = m["missing"]
	m["x"] = 1
	fmt.Println(m)
}
question: What happens when this program runs?
options: Panics on the read of m[missing]
Panics on the write to m[x]
explain: Reading from a nil map returns the zero value without panicking, but writing to a nil map triggers a runtime panic. Reviewers must always ensure maps are initialized with make or a literal before any assignment.

code:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	a := make([]int, 0, 2)
	b := append(a, 1)
	c := append(a, 2)
	fmt.Println(b[0], c[0])
}
question: What is the output and why is this dangerous?
options: 2 2 - both b and c alias a's backing array because cap was available
explain: When the destination has spare capacity, append writes into the same backing array, so b and c share storage and the second append overwrites the first. Reviewers must flag fan-out append patterns from a shared base slice as aliasing bugs.

question: What does this print and why?
options: true - nil pointer is a nil interface
false - interface holds (type=*myErr, value=nil), so it is not nil
explain: An interface is nil only when both its type and value are nil; returning a typed nil pointer produces a non-nil error interface that holds a nil pointer. Reviewers must require functions to return nil explicitly rather than returning a typed-nil variable.

question: What does errors.Is return and why?
options: true - %s and %w are equivalent for Is
false - %s formats the message but does not wrap, so the chain is broken
explain: Only the %w verb stores the wrapped error in the returned wrapError, enabling errors.Is/As to traverse the chain. Reviewers must replace %s with %w anywhere an error is embedded in a formatted message that callers may inspect.

question: What does the reviewer warn about?
options: d*1000 silently overflows int64 nanoseconds and wraps to a negative duration
time.Hour is a float and loses precision
Compile error: cannot multiply Duration by int
explain: time.Duration is an int64 nanosecond count, capping at roughly 292 years; multiplying a 300-day duration by 1000 overflows and wraps. Reviewers must bounds-check arithmetic on Duration values from external input.

question: What is the role of the turbofish here?
options: It is mandatory; without it the program fails to compile
It disambiguates the collect target type since the return is already annotated; here it is redundant but legal
It forces a Vec allocation in static memory
It is a lifetime annotation for the iterator
explain: Tests understanding of type inference for collect. The return type already constrains B = Vec<u32>, so ::<Vec<_>> is harmless redundancy. Reviewer skill: spot unnecessary turbofish that obscures intent.

question: What does the pattern [first, rest @ ..] do?
options: Borrows the first element as &i32 and binds rest to a Vec<i32>
Binds first to &i32 and rest to &[i32] (the remaining slice)
Moves first out of the slice; rest is a slice iterator
Pattern is invalid; subslice patterns require nightly
explain: Slice patterns bind elements by reference when matching on &[T]; the @ .. captures the rest as a subslice reference. Reviewer skill: read slice patterns correctly.

question: What happens in debug vs release builds?
options: Debug build panics with overflow check; release build wraps silently (defined two's-complement wrap)
explain: Default integer arithmetic panics on overflow in debug and wraps in release per Rust's overflow policy. Reviewers should flag implicit overflow and recommend explicit checked_/wrapping_/saturating_ methods.

question: What does nan_check return and why?
options: true: every value equals itself in Rust
false: NaN is not equal to anything including itself by IEEE-754; use f64::is_nan instead
Panics at runtime on the comparison
Compile error: f64 does not implement PartialEq
explain: IEEE-754 mandates NaN != NaN; this is why f64 implements only PartialEq. Use f64::is_nan for identification. Reviewer skill: catch equality-on-floats bugs.

question: What property does const fn give square here?
options: It guarantees compile-time evaluation for every call
It allows calls in const contexts (like const items) while still permitting normal runtime calls
It makes the function unsafe to call at runtime
It implies the function is inlined
explain: const fn means the function may be evaluated at compile time when used in a const context, but it remains a regular function at runtime. Reviewer skill: pick const fn for APIs needed in const initializers.

question: What is the benefit of this index design?
options: Partial index excludes inactive rows, producing a smaller, faster index that only covers the hot working set queried with active = true
Partial indexes are always slower than full indexes
The WHERE clause on the index is ignored by the planner
It prevents inserts into inactive rows
explain: Reviewing partial index design: WHERE clauses on indexes shrink the structure to relevant rows, lowering maintenance cost and improving cache locality. Reviewers should confirm the query predicate is implied by the index predicate so the planner can use it.

code:
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email_lower ON users(LOWER(email));
SELECT id FROM users WHERE LOWER(email) = 'alice@example.com';
question: Why is this expression index appropriate?
options: LOWER() in WHERE prevents any index use, so this index is useless
An expression index on LOWER(email) lets the planner match the LOWER(email) predicate; a plain index on email would not be used here
This index would only work for ILIKE
explain: Reviewing expression indexes: when a query wraps a column in a function, the planner needs an index built on the identical expression. Reviewers should match function calls in WHERE with corresponding expression indexes.

code:
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_cov ON orders(customer_id) INCLUDE (status, total);
SELECT status, total FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 100;
question: What advantage does INCLUDE give here?
options: INCLUDE columns are part of the index key and used for filtering
Covering index with INCLUDE lets the query be answered purely from the index (index-only scan) without visiting the heap
INCLUDE forces the use of a hash index
explain: Reviewing covering indexes: INCLUDE stores non-key payload in leaf pages, enabling index-only scans for queries projecting only those columns. Reviewers should ensure visibility map is fresh so the scan avoids heap fetches.

code:
SELECT id, title
FROM posts
WHERE id > 10000
ORDER BY id ASC
LIMIT 20;
question: Why is this keyset pagination preferable to OFFSET 10000?
options: OFFSET is faster than keyset for large offsets
Keyset pagination uses an index range scan to jump directly to the next page in O(log n) per page, whereas OFFSET 10000 forces scanning and discarding 10000 rows
Keyset only works on small tables
explain: Reviewing pagination strategy: OFFSET N scans and discards N rows on every page, so deep pages get linearly slower. Keyset is O(log n) per page via the btree.

code:
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_stat_statements;
SELECT query, calls, total_exec_time FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC LIMIT 10;
question: What is pg_stat_statements used for in review?
options: It aggregates normalized query stats (calls, total/mean exec time, rows, buffers) across the cluster, letting reviewers identify the slowest or most frequent queries to focus optimization
explain: Reviewing workload-level perf: pg_stat_statements is the canonical extension for top-N slow query analysis with normalized text. Reviewers should sort by total_exec_time (impact) rather than mean_exec_time alone.
